finalAssistantMessages.text: The live root is now returning the important gateway header: `X-Robots-Tag: noindex, nofollow`. I’m checking the HTML meta tag and one nested asset too, because image/search indexing can involve both.

Yes, it’s working now on the live URL: [eraofmeat.com/ipfs/QmYqZ...](https://eraofmeat.com/ipfs/QmYqZVrS8B57TJz69hQoLcNFqyDNe8u9EKBeXh7u3zhRKi/#/) I checked the root page, `index.html`, and a nested image asset. All are returning: ```http X-Robots-Tag: noindex, nofollow ``` The nested image also keeps the header on range requests (`206`), so image indexing should be covered too. One note: this old static site HTML itself does not contain the `<meta name="robots">` tag, which is expected unless the site is regenerated. But the gateway header is live and is the important part for existing already-published IPFS roots. Google may still show cached/indexed results until it recrawls and processes

Yes, the images inside that static site should be covered too. I checked one of the image URLs under the site root, and it returns: ```http X-Robots-Tag: noindex, nofollow ``` That matters because Google says `X-Robots-Tag` works for non-HTML resources like image files, not just pages: [Google docs](https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/block-indexing). Small caveat: this protects images when accessed through this static-site path: ```text /ipfs/QmYqZ.../content/image.jpg ``` If the exact same image CID is exposed somewhere else as its own standalone `/ipfs/bafy...` URL without this header, Google could treat that as a separate URL.
